title: Graphs
description: Master C# graph representation with Dictionary<int,List<int>> adjacency lists, DFS, BFS, and connected components for coding interviews.

sections.title: Adjacency List Representation
sections.explanation: Represent graphs using `Dictionary<int, List<int>>` where each key is a node and its value is the list of neighbors. For undirected graphs, add both directions. This is the most common graph representation in interviews—it's space-efficient O(V + E) and provides O(1) neighbor lookup. Build from edge lists: for each edge (u, v), add v to graph[u].

exercise.prompt: Build a directed graph from edges: (0→1), (0→2), (1→3), (2→3), (3→4). Print each node and its neighbors list. Print the total number of nodes and edges.

validation.message: Adjacency list for directed graph, 5 nodes, 5 edges.
Use Dictionary<int, List<int>> for adjacency list with Add for edges.

exercise.hint: For directed: only add edge[1] to graph[edge[0]]. Ensure all nodes (including 4) have entries.

sections.title: Graph DFS: Recursive
sections.explanation: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. Use a `HashSet<int>` to track visited nodes (prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs). Pattern: mark node visited, process it, recurse on unvisited neighbors. DFS is used for: path finding, cycle detection, topological sort, and connected components.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-4, 3-4. Write a DFS that collects the path from node 0. Print all visited nodes in DFS order, separated by spaces. Also write a `bool HasPath(graph, start, end, visited)` method and test if path exists from 0 to 4.

validation.message: DFS order from 0, then True for path from 0 to 4.
Use HashSet for visited tracking, implement recursive DFS and HasPath.

exercise.hint: DFS: add to visited and order, recurse on unvisited neighbors. HasPath: if start == end return true, DFS with early return.

sections.title: Graph BFS: Shortest Path
sections.explanation: BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Use Queue + HashSet for visited. Pattern: enqueue start with distance 0, dequeue and process, enqueue unvisited neighbors with distance + 1. BFS guarantees the first time you reach a node is via the shortest path. Track distances with a Dictionary<int, int>.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 1-4, 2-5, 3-5. Find shortest distances from node 0 to all other nodes using BFS. Print each as "node: distance", sorted by node number.

validation.message: Shortest distances from node 0 to all nodes.
Use Queue for BFS, track distances in Dictionary.

exercise.hint: dist[start] = 0, enqueue start. While queue not empty: dequeue, for each neighbor not in dist: set distance, enqueue.

sections.title: Connected Components
sections.explanation: Find connected components by iterating all nodes: for each unvisited node, run DFS/BFS to discover its component. Count the number of components. This pattern is used for: number of islands, friend groups, network connectivity. Time: O(V + E) to visit all nodes and edges once.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph with 7 nodes (0-6) and edges: 0-1, 0-2, 3-4, 5-6. Count and print the number of connected components. Then print which nodes belong to each component (as sorted lists).

validation.message: Use HashSet for visited, iterate all nodes to find components.

exercise.hint: For each unvisited node, run DFS to collect all nodes in that component. Sort each component before printing.
